Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
October 
■6′  ■99■
Evet Abt
chief Deputy city Attorney
city of san Jose
151 West Hission Street
San Jose, cA 95110
Re3 Your Request for Advice
our File No. r-91-4 3 6
Dear Ms, Abt:
This is in response to your request for further advice
concernj.ng the issues raised in the Abt Advice Letter, No. A-91-
361, which was issued on August 20, t99L. Both the first 
Abt
Advice Letter and your nost recent letter are seeking advice on
oehalf of san Jose Airport Conmissioner Robert Thompson with
respect to the conmissioner's respons ibi l ities under the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the politicaL Reforrn Act (the rrlggtrl.l'
Since you are seeking general guidance with respect to a series of
decisions of the san Jose Airport cornniqsion, vre are treating your
request as one for infornal assistance.2
OUESTTONS
1. Does the status of the Public Affairs Council as a
nonprofit civic league pursuant to Section 501(c) (4) of the
fnternal Revenue Code change the analysis contained in the first
advice letter?
2. To what extent are decisions concerning the San Jose
Airport's Haster Plan severable, rrhere a decision on the
developnent of any one type of aviation use at the San Jose
1 cor".rr."nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. AIl statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherlrise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 CaLifornia Code of Regulations Section
18000, et ses. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
imrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Governnent Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
section 18329 (c) (3 ) . )
428 J Street,Suite 800 ● P.o.Box 807 0 Sacramento CA 95804Ю807 ● (916)322´ 5660


Our Flle No. I-91-436
Page 2
Airport, such as air cargo, would necessarily re-su1t in a decrease
or ireciusion of developrnent for other uses, such as passenger air
carrier facilities?
CONCLUSIONS
1. ttlncoBerr does not include reirnbursement for travel
expenses and per dien received froro a bona fide educational,
acidenic, or Lharitable organization. since the Public Affairs
Council is not a bona fide educational, academic, or charitable
organization, but a nonprofit civic organization, the exception in
se6tion 82030(b) (2) will not apply. Thus, tickets received by
connissioner ihornpson fron the puuric Affairs council rnust be
disclosed as additional income fron the Public Affairs Council'
ltoreover, Connissioner Thonpson nay not make, . participate in
making or in any way attenpt to use his official position to
influ6nce a governnental decision which will have a reasonabJ'y
foreseeable iaterial financial effect on the PubIic Affairs
Council.
2. GeneraIIy, each decision concerning the san.Jose Airport
Master plan Eust Le analyzed independently to determine. if.there
wi]I be a foreseeable roaterial financial effect on commlssloner
thornpson's econonic interests. However, if the decisions
conclrning the developuent of one type of aviation use at the san
loie eirp5rt rroul.d neiessarily resuit in a decrease or preclusion
of devel-opnent for other uses, the san Jose Airport litaster PIan
decisions-nay be too interrelated to be treated separately' If
the decisionS are interrelated and Connissioner Thonpson has a
conflict of lnterest with respect to one asPect of the Airport
Master Plan, the connissioner nay not participate in alternate
decisions concerning the san Jose Airport l'laster Plan'
FAqTS
The facts are substantially the same as in your first advice
request.
Cournissioner Robert Thornpson is an appointed
cornmissioner on the san Jose Airport Commission
(the "connissionrr) and serves as the comnission's
chairrnan. fn addition, the commissioner serves in
his private capacity as president of the PubIic
Affairs council (the 'rcouncil"), a nonprofit
corporation established as a tax-exempt
organization under...the Revenue and Taxation code.
The council was established to pronote a
strong and hea).thy economy in Santa clara and San
Benito Counties consistent vith environnental
concerns in the area. The council is controlled by
a thirty-nenber board of directors which includes
corporate nenbers. The board deternines the
rnenbership of the organization and sets the


Our File No. I-9■
-436
Page 3
compensation to be paid to Coromissioner Thonpson as
president. Merobership dues are used for
operational expenses, including salarles. The
council actively lobbies the tegislature in
furtherance of its policy goals. As president of
the council, connissioner Thonpson is active in
recruiting ner., EeDbers for the organization.
American Airlines, Inc. is currently seeking
to join the council as a voting :nember of the board
of directors. The airline has proposed providing
free transportation for the president and other
council officials in lieu of paying nembership
dues. American Airlines, Inc. is also a rnajor
tenant at the San Jose International Airport and
issues affecting the operations of aII airlines,
inctuding American Airlines, Inc., regularly corne
before the Airport conEission for review and
recornmendation.
Additionally, another nenber of the council's
board of directors is also an investor in one of
San Jose Airport's tenants, the San Jose Jet
center, Inc. Matters affecting the tenants of the
airport, including the San Jose Jet center, Inc.,
regularly cone before the Airport coronission for
review and reconnendation.
In your request for further asslstance you have clarified
that the Public Affairs Council, is a nonprofit tax-exenrpt civic
league under Section 501(c) (4) of the Internal Revenue Code. You
hav6 asked how this lnforrnation nay change the analysis used in
the initlal $! Ietter.
You also stated you are concerned about the disgua I if ication
responsibil ities of connissioner Thonpson with respect to a series
of decisions about the san Jose AirPort Master PIan. The master
plan involves a variety of airport developnent issues, including
the developnent of air-cargo, general aviation, and passenger air
carrier facilities. 
You stated that decision on the development
of any one type of aviation use would necessarily result in a
decrelse or preclusion of developrnent for other uses. You believe
that the council will take public policy positions on the type of
devetopment that should occur at the airport.
ANALYSIS
1. Econonic fnterests
As discussed in the first Abt letter, Commissioner Thonpson
is prohibited fron naking, participating in making, or otherwise
using his official position to influence a governrnental decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable naterial financial
effect, distinguishable froro the effect on the public generally,


Our Flle No. I-9■
-436
Page 4
on the officlat or a nenber of hls or her iDmediate family or on
any source of incone of S25o or llore within the 12 months prior to
tha tiEe when a decision is made. (Section 87100,' Section
87103 (c) . )
We advised that the salary frou the Pub1ic Affairs council
(the rrcouncilrr) was ninconetr as defined in the Act which created a
potentially diiqualifying econornic interest in the council'.
ilowever, we also advised that, since the council was a tax exempt
nonprofit charitable organization under Section 501(c) (3) of the
lnt-ernal Revenue code, the tickets provided to the connissioner
for use on council business would bL exenpted from the definition
of irinconerr under section 82030(b) (2).
Section 82030(b) (2) Provides that rrinconel does not include:
Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
dien received fron a state, local, or federal
governnent agency and reinbursenent for travel
ixpenses and per diern received from a bona. fide
edircational, lcademic' or charitable orqanization.
Enphasis added.
Since the council is not a bona fide educational, academic,
or charitable organization, but rather a nonprofit civic
organization, tha exception ln Sectlon 82030(b) (2) .wi11 not apply
and th" tickets should be disclosed as additional incone fron the
council. Horrever, since the council was already treated as a
source of incoue io the cornmissioner, the only changes that will
result fron the new lnfonuation concerninq the status of the
council is linited to disclosure and the quantity of incone
consldered for disqua). ification PurPoses. This does not change
the conclusion in €he first lettLr, that the commissioner may not
rnake, participate in naking or in any way attenpt.to use. his
otfiiiif 
position to influ6nce a governnintal decision which will
have a relsonably foreseeable nattrial financial effect on the
council.
2. Nexus
As we discussed in the first request for advice, since the
conmissioner has an economic interest in the council, nanely, the
incone he derives fron the council, he nust disqualify himself
fron any decision in which the council is directly before the
Airport Connission as an applicant or the subject of the decision,
or any decisions in which there exists a nexus between the purpose
for rrhich the official receives income and the governmental
decision.3 A nexus exists if the cornrnissioner receives incone to
3 Th. cornmissioner is also required to disqualify
any decisions which will have an indirect naterial
effect on the council. (Regulation 18702.5.)
hinself fron
financial


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-436
Page 5
achieve a goal or purpose whlch hrould be achieved, defeated,
aided, or hindered by the governrtrental decision. (Regulation
La702.1(a) (1). )
We advised that since commissioner Thornpson receives incorue
frotn the council, a nexus could exist in any commission decision
which would further the policy positions of the council. we
stated:
For example, in the Best Advice Letter (suDra)
we advised: trif [the nonprofit] took a position on
a particular proposal before [the agtency], your
dilqualification on the natter hrould be required.r'
In the Cornelius Advice Letter, No. A-82-104 (copy
enclosed) we advised: ttlf you vote on a decision to
provide a grant to the [nonprofit], you are
acconplishing as a public official what you are
paid Lo do in your position with the [nonprof it].I'
In the Scheidiq Advice Letter, No. A-82-212
(copy enclosed) we advised that the nayor of
concord who received incone fron the Bay Area
Councll ('rBAcx) for work irlth BAc's Housing
Advocacy Program was disqualified fron housing
decisions beiore the concord city council on which
BAc had taken a particular position, had a specific
policy or position, or had a oeneral oolicY lhat
Llearlv inolied a specific result on a decision.
You are concerned that the council rrill take a position on an
aspect of the Airport Master PIan. You have asked whether the
vaiious decisions relating to the Airport t'laster Plan nay be
separated such that the conuissioner roay participate in decisions
on- which the council has not taken a policy position. We have
advised in past letters that the general rule is that every
decision nust be analyzed independently to deterrnine if there will
be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's
financial interest. (fn re Owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops' 77i Kilian
Advice Letter, No. A-89-522; Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-
343. )
However, in sone cases, decisions rnay be too interrelated to
be considered separately. ff the resolution of one decision wilI
effectively determine the result of the other decision, the
councilnember nust disqualify hinself as to both. For exarnple, if
a decision is to place a ner./ building on one of two locations, the
decision to place the building at one location necessarily decides
whether the building is to be placed at the alternate location.
If an official has a financial interest in the alternate location,
he nay not participate in the decision. conversely, where the
decision involves the construction of two buildings on one of
several Lots, and the official has an interest in one of the lots,
once a building is placed on his lot without his participation, he


Our Fi■e No. I-9■-436
Page 6
I trust this
have any further
to contact De at
nay partici.pate in the decision to place the other building on one
of the other lots '
While the Airport Uaster Plan nay involve a variety of
airport developuent issues, including the development of .air
cargo, general aviation, and passenger air carrier facilities, 
you
havi stited that aII the decislons witl be interrelated. If these
decisions are in fact interrelated, such that one decision
necessarily decides the others, the comrnissioner roay not
participate in any of the decisions.
However, if you detennine that the decisions are separable
and that a decision on one matter nill not effectively result in a
final deternination as to another of the pending decisions, the
cornrnissioner nay parti.cipate in the other decisions provided:
1. The decisions fron rrhich the cornrnissioner is disqualified
are decided independent of aII the other decisions.
2. The decisions fron vhich the comnissioner is disqualified
are considered first, and a final decision reached.by the
cornnission lrithout the conmissloner participating in any way.
3. FinaLly, once a decision has been nade on the issues for
which the connissioner is disqualified, the other decisions vhich
the connissioner will participate ln will not result in a
reopening of , 9L-iJL3Ey-J3y-3lf.E!, the prior decisions from which
the- corunissioner was disgualified. (Huffaker Advice Letter,
suora . )
